region_of_origin: Guangdong (Canton), China; widely served in Singapore
food_subgroup: Pre-fried crispy egg noodles softened in thick cornflour-thickened seafood-egg gravy

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no dedicated concept for Yee Mee / Yi Mian. Closest parent is a fried-then-braised egg noodle dish with seafood gravy. No URI assigned to avoid fabrication.
ontology.foodex2_description: Pre-fried crispy egg noodles braised in thick seafood-egg gravy with fish cake, prawn, and squid
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: A0CFA (Egg noodle dish) is the closest defensible FoodEx2 parent. No exact code exists for this specific preparation.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for this specific dish. Not assigned to avoid fabrication.

main_ingredients: pre-fried egg noodles (wheat flour, egg, palm oil)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of braised yee mee at a zi char stall or Cantonese restaurant is approximately 400g, comprising roughly 100g pre-fried egg noodles, 200g thick seafood gravy, and 100g mixed seafood and vegetables.

nutrient_source.title: Yee Mee Braised / Yi Mian — HPB Singapore Foodo
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database for braised yee mee / yi mian. Values represent the composite dish as served, including pre-fried egg noodles and thick seafood gravy. Minor micronutrients (copper, manganese, vitamin K) supplemented from enriched_json estimates at confidence 0.9. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and water content are not reported by the HPB Foodo source for this dish; these fields are set to -1 to indicate not reported by source, as documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values — Egg noodles (pre-fried, wheat-based)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for braised yee mee / yi mian. GI of 68 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for pre-fried wheat-based egg noodles (GI range ~65–75). The pre-frying process, inclusion of fats (palm oil, sesame oil), and protein from seafood moderate the glycaemic response relative to plain boiled wheat noodles. GL per 400g serving = (68 × 66g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 44.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content (520 mg/100g) from oyster sauce and soy sauce in the thick gravy; a 400g serving delivers approximately 2080 mg sodium, exceeding the WHO daily recommended maximum of 2000 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit braised yee mee to a small portion (≤200g, ~1040 mg sodium) or avoid it altogether; request the sauce on the side and choose lower-sodium accompaniments to stay within daily sodium targets.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycaemic load per serving (GL 44.9 for 400g) driven by refined wheat egg noodles (GI ~68) and cornflour-thickened gravy, delivering approximately 66g available carbohydrate per plate.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with type 2 diabetes should limit braised yee mee to a half-portion (≤200g, GL ~22.5), pair with additional seafood protein to blunt the glycaemic response, and request less gravy to reduce the cornflour starch load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from palm oil used in pre-frying (2.5g/100g; ~10g per 400g serving), combined with high sodium (2080 mg/400g serving) and cholesterol (48 mg/100g; ~192 mg/400g serving), presents a composite cardiovascular risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit braised yee mee to a small portion (≤200g), choose preparations with less gravy to reduce sodium and saturated fat intake, and balance the meal with non-starchy vegetables to improve the overall dietary fat and sodium profile.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Seafood components (prawns, squid) provide selenium (18 mcg/100g) and zinc (1.2 mg/100g) with anti-inflammatory properties; saturated fat from palm oil is moderate at 2.5g/100g.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Braised yee mee is generally acceptable for patients with rheumatoid arthritis in moderate portions; the seafood provides beneficial selenium and zinc, though the high sodium warrants caution in those with concurrent hypertension.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Yee Mee Braised (干烧伊面 / 干燒伊麵, also known as Yi Mian or E-fu Noodles) is a classic Cantonese dish widely served at Singapore zi char stalls and Cantonese restaurants. The defining ingredient is the pre-fried egg noodle cake — a disc of wheat-and-egg noodles that has been deep-fried in palm oil and dried, giving it a porous, spongy, golden-brown texture with a nutty aroma. When braised in hot thick seafood gravy, the crispy noodle cake rapidly absorbs the liquid and transforms from crunchy to silky and chewy within minutes. The thick gravy is made from chicken-prawn stock generously thickened with cornflour and seasoned with oyster sauce, light soy sauce, and sesame oil, producing a glossy, savoury, umami-rich sauce. The dish is typically filled with prawns, squid, sliced fish cake, choy sum, and sometimes egg ribbons, providing a balance of seafood protein and vegetable texture. Culturally, yee mee is a longevity symbol served at Chinese wedding banquets and birthday celebrations, as the long noodles represent long life. Nutritionally, the dish provides 148 kcal/100g with 8.5g protein and 16.5g carbohydrate. The primary clinical concerns are very high sodium (520 mg/100g; ~2080 mg per 400g plate) from the oyster-soy gravy, a high glycaemic load per serving (GL ~44.9 for 400g), and saturated fat from the palm-oil pre-frying (2.5g/100g). Seafood components contribute beneficial selenium (18 mcg/100g) and vitamin B12 (0.8 mcg/100g). This dish is not recommended for patients with hypertension or type 2 diabetes in standard serving sizes.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo (Health Promotion Board, Singapore), the primary approved source for Singapore dishes. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry for pre-fried wheat egg noodles (GI ~65–75), with estimation basis clearly documented. Minor micronutrients (copper, manganese, vitamin K) supplemented from enriched_json estimates at confidence 0.9. All mandatory multilingual names present for a Singaporean dish.
notes: All core macros are non-zero and nutritionally plausible for a braised egg noodle dish with seafood gravy. No zero-macro justification required. GI is an analogous estimate (not a direct measurement) from the University of Sydney GI Database for pre-fried wheat egg noodles; the estimation basis is documented in gi_source. Monounsaturated fat (monounsaturated_fat_g = -1): not reported by HPB Singapore Foodo for this dish; value set to -1 per G3 convention for source-unreported nutrients. Polyunsaturated fat (polyunsaturated_fat_g = -1): not reported by HPB Singapore Foodo for this dish; value set to -1 per G3 convention for source-unreported nutrients. Water content (water_g = -1): not reported by HPB Singapore Foodo for this dish; value set to -1 per G3 convention for source-unreported nutrients. Copper (0.08 mg/100g), manganese (0.15 mg/100g), and vitamin K (15 mcg/100g) are enriched_json estimates at confidence 0.9 and should be verified against a primary source in a future update. The flat aliases list and aliases_by_language have been cross-checked for consistency; every string in aliases_by_language appears verbatim in the flat aliases list. Hindi local name added to satisfy Singaporean dish multilingual requirement (G6). Duplicate portions in input (two identical 250g entries) consolidated; 400g plate retained as default serving size.
